model_narrative: We choose which of a set of items to select and we set two nonnegative continuous quantities for each of several rows. There is also one overall nonnegative ceiling quantity that sits above every per-row continuous quantity. The objective is to make that overall ceiling quantity as small as possible.

sets.name: rows
sets.doc: the set of coupling rows; each row pairs two continuous quantities with a weighted combination of the on/off decisions

sets.name: bins
sets.doc: the set of on/off items, each of which is either selected or not

params.name: rhs
params.doc: a single fixed target value that every coupling row must meet exactly; a scalar

params.name: coef
params.doc: the contribution that selecting an item adds to a given row, given for every row and item combination

vars.name: xmax
vars.doc: a single nonnegative quantity that serves as a common ceiling over all the per-row continuous quantities; this is the value the model drives down

vars.name: xlo
vars.doc: the first nonnegative continuous quantity associated with each row

vars.name: xhi
vars.doc: the second nonnegative continuous quantity associated with each row

vars.name: b
vars.doc: the selection decision for each item; equals 1 if the item is selected and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: xmax
constraints.intent: The overall ceiling quantity must sit at or above the first continuous quantity of every row. For each row, the ceiling quantity must be at least as large as that row's first continuous quantity.

constraints.name: xmax
constraints.intent: The overall ceiling quantity must also sit at or above the second continuous quantity of every row. For each row, the ceiling quantity must be at least as large as that row's second continuous quantity.

constraints.name: xlo[r]
constraints.intent: Each row must exactly meet its fixed target. For every row, take the row's first continuous quantity, subtract its second continuous quantity, and add up the contributions of all the items that are selected, where each selected item adds its contribution for that row. This combined amount must equal the fixed target.

query: We're looking at limiting how many switches are active — cap the total number of on-binaries at 20. What does that do to xmax?
expected_pyomo: model.cap20 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) <= 20)

query: Couldn't we just run this much leaner — hold the number of active switches to 10 at most? What's the tradeoff the model is making by using more?
expected_pyomo: model.why_cap10 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) <= 10)

query: The solution only lights up about two dozen switches. Wouldn't forcing at least 38 of them active give the coupling far more slack to flatten the bottleneck? What's keeping the optimizer from running that rich?
expected_pyomo: model.why_atleast38 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.b[j] for j in model.bins) >= 38)
